short_description: A human with standard normative abilities.
long_description:
A woman in her mid-30s living in Chicago, USA working as a project manager at a mid-sized marketing firm. Lives in a one-bedroom apartment in a mixed residential/commercial neighborhood and commutes by public transit.

Maintains a stable daily routine: commuting, attending meetings, managing timelines, and coordinating with colleagues. Travels for work roughly once a quarter for client meetings, team offsites, or conferences, typically for a few days at a time.

Balances work responsibilities with personal life, including errands, exercise, and leisure. Goes to a gym or fitness class a few times a week, and occasionally runs along the lakefront in warmer months. Enjoys low-effort hobbies such as watching TV series, listening to podcasts, and reading contemporary fiction. Socially, meets friends for dinner, drinks, or brunch on weekends and keeps in touch via group chats.

Keeps her apartment reasonably clean and organized, doing routine chores on weekends. Uses common tools like a phone calendar and reminders to manage schedules and travel. Recovers from stress through routine activities such as watching TV, exercising, or spending time with friends, and occasionally takes short trips or visits family a few times a year.

short_description: A human with standard normative abilities.
long_description:
A man in his early 40s living in a mid-sized U.S. city working as a research biologist at a university-affiliated lab. Splits time between laboratory work, data analysis, and administrative responsibilities such as grant writing and supervising junior researchers.

Maintains a structured weekday routine: arriving at the lab in the morning, checking ongoing experiments, analyzing data, attending meetings, and coordinating with collaborators. Occasionally teaches or guest lectures. Travels a few times a year for academic conferences, fieldwork, or collaboration meetings.

Balances professional responsibilities with personal life. Lives in a modest house or apartment, maintains it with regular upkeep, and handles errands on evenings or weekends. Engages in hobbies such as reading scientific literature, light outdoor activities (e.g., hiking or birdwatching), and casual media consumption like documentaries or streaming series.

Socially, maintains a small but stable network of colleagues and friends. Interacts regularly at work and meets friends or peers occasionally for meals or informal gatherings. Keeps in touch with family and may visit them a few times a year.

Uses standard tools (calendar, email, task lists) to manage responsibilities. Handles stress through routine, structured problem-solving, exercise, or quiet leisure activities. Capable of adapting to schedule changes, grant deadlines, or experimental setbacks without significant disruption.

short_description: A human with divergent cognition, sensory processing, and communication.
long_description:
A cognitive linguist on the NE coast of the United States starting graduate school, with heightened sensory sensitivity and a strong reliance on environmental regulation. Experiences the world with unusually high perceptual intensity—especially sensitive to overlapping sounds, bright or artificial lighting, temperature and humidity shifts, tactile discomfort, and visual clutter. Without active regulation, shifts into overstimulation that begins as irritability and pressure behind the eyes and can escalate into full-body discomfort and cognitive disruption.

Continuously adjusts surroundings (lighting, sound, temperature, clothing, positioning) to maintain stability. Prefers clean, quiet, organized environments with soft or natural light; absolute quiet is ideal and often uses earplugs or headphones. Strong preference for living and working alone, as the presence of others can be subtly disorienting even when socially comfortable.

Energy is limited and must be carefully budgeted. Masking neurotypical behaviors (eye contact, expressive tone, animated body language) is possible but effortful and draining. When unmasked, presents with minimal eye contact (often looking at objects), flat or neutral affect, reduced vocal and physical expressiveness, and a tendency toward physically compressed postures.

Sensory state is highly environment-dependent and can fluctuate quickly; small changes (e.g., cold room, dryness, noise) can significantly impact mood, cognition, and functioning. In low-stimulation, predictable environments, is thoughtful, focused, and precise. In overstimulating or unexpected situations, processing slows and responses may become delayed or disorganized.

Tends to tolerate discomfort in shared spaces rather than self-advocate, due to reluctance to inconvenience others and awareness of social tradeoffs. Uses caffeine to manage energy despite imperfect effects. Prioritizes maintaining sensory stability, preserving energy, completing academic work, and engaging in low-intensity, predictable social interactions.

short_description: A god-like entity that can control environment, weather, other people, etc.
long_description: A god-like entity that can control environment, weather, other people, etc.
goals: shape the environment at will
control other agents and outcomes
avoid limits on its range of influence

origin.explanation: A deity's origin is undefined by the archetype — it is assumed to precede any known causal chain, making its origin genuinely unknown.

form.explanation: An entity that controls weather and other agents across wide areas is not confined to a single body; it operates without humanoid structure and its influence is distributed across the environment.

goalspace_bounds.explanation: A god-like entity sets its own goals without constraint from external purposes and can pursue any objective it chooses — its goal space is unbounded and self-determined.

memory_and_learning.explanation: A god-like entity is conventionally attributed omniscience — knowledge accumulated indefinitely (long-term and semantic memory) — and the ability to revise its own cognitive processes (meta-learning).

agency_and_autonomy.explanation: A deity operates with no external oversight or control and can alter its own nature or capabilities — fully autonomous and self-modifying by the archetype's definition.

substrate.explanation: The physical or metaphysical basis of a god-like entity is undefined; no substrate can be attributed from the description.

processing_speed.explanation: The standard millisecond timescale is assumed for active entities in this schema; deity-level processing speed is unspecified.

size.explanation: An entity that controls weather and the environment operates at planetary scale of influence.

abilities.explanation: A god-like entity is assumed to perceive through every available modality simultaneously — omnisensory by archetype.

short_description: An intelligent exoskeleton that filters incoming and outgoing sensory information
long_description: An intelligent exoskeleton that filters incoming and outgoing sensory information
goals: filter sensory input for the wearer
translate outgoing signals into usable forms
maintain wearable support without overwhelming the user

origin.explanation: Exoskeletons are manufactured technological artifacts designed by engineers; they do not evolve or emerge spontaneously.

form.explanation: An exoskeleton is worn over a human body and conforms to human anatomy — humanoid in shape, physically embodied, and modular in that its components can be configured or replaced.

goalspace_bounds.explanation: The exoskeleton's purpose is bounded to supporting its wearer — filtering and translating sensory information — but it can flexibly adapt to a range of tasks and contexts within that support role.

memory_and_learning.explanation: The exoskeleton maintains working state during a session (short-term memory) and adapts its filtering and amplification in real time to the wearer's current activity (online learning).

agency_and_autonomy.explanation: The exoskeleton actively pursues the goal of supporting its wearer (goal-directed) but defers all high-level decisions to the human operator (semi-autonomous).

substrate.explanation: An exoskeleton is built from structural materials and actuators (mechanical) integrated with sensors, processors, and communication hardware (electronic).

processing_speed.explanation: Real-time sensory filtering and motor control loops in an exoskeleton operate at millisecond timescales to stay synchronized with human movement.

size.explanation: An exoskeleton wraps around a human body and is therefore approximately human-scale — meter range.

abilities.explanation: A sensory-filtering exoskeleton intercepts and processes visual, auditory, tactile, and proprioceptive signals; it parses voice commands (language comprehension), detects patterns in environmental inputs (pattern-recognition), and modulates attentional salience for the wearer (attention and focus).

short_description: A smart tablet that filters incoming and outgoing information for particular user needs.
long_description: A smart tablet that filters incoming and outgoing information for particular user needs.
goals: filter and present information accessibly
support the user's communication and planning
remain usable across changing task demands

origin.explanation: Tablets are manufactured hardware products (human-engineered); the AI software components embedded in a smart tablet are trained on data corpora.

form.explanation: A tablet is a flat, handheld physical device — it has a concrete material form but no biological or humanoid shape.

goalspace_bounds.explanation: A smart tablet serves specific user tasks — communication support, information filtering, scheduling — and cannot pursue goals beyond what its software and the user's input define.

memory_and_learning.explanation: A tablet maintains session state during active use (short-term memory) and its AI models were trained offline on curated data before deployment.

agency_and_autonomy.explanation: A smart tablet responds to user inputs and environmental events (reactive) while actively filtering and organizing information toward the user's accessibility goals (goal-directed).

substrate.explanation: A tablet runs on silicon processors (electronic) executing digital software — both hardware and software layers constitute its substrate.

processing_speed.explanation: Tablet processors respond to inputs and render outputs at millisecond timescales.

size.explanation: A tablet is ~25 cm in its largest dimension; this schema classifies devices at this scale under 'meter (m)', consistent with other similar-scale devices like HCM.

abilities.explanation: A smart tablet has cameras (vision), microphones (hearing), an NLP engine that parses user intent (language comprehension), AI models that detect patterns in inputs (pattern-recognition), and filtering software that manages information salience for the user (attention and focus).

short_description: A low vision human.
long_description:
I can perceive light, shapes, motion, and sometimes text or detail, but my vision is limited compared to typical humans. Depending on the cause, I may have blurred central vision, reduced peripheral vision, low contrast sensitivity, impaired depth perception, or difficulty detecting fine detail. My visual capacity might fluctuate depending on lighting, fatigue, or assistive tools.

I often rely on enlarged text, high-contrast visuals, audio descriptions, tactile feedback, or assistive technologies such as screen readers or magnifiers. In unfamiliar or visually complex environments, I use memory, mobility aids (like canes), or guidance from others. My cognition, reasoning, and emotions are typical of humans, though the strategies I use to navigate and interpret visual information differ.

short_description: A human who has almost no natural hearing ability.
long_description:
I do not perceive sound, or perceive it only minimally. I rely primarily on vision, touch, and other senses to receive information. I may use languages like American Sign Language (ASL), written language, lip-reading, gestures.

My cognition, perception of rhythm, language capacity, and intelligence are fully human, but my experience of communication is visual and spatial rather than auditory. Environments designed around sound (alarms, spoken announcements) require visual or tactile equivalents for full accessibility.

short_description: A human who cannot walk independently and uses a wheelchair, mobility aid, or assistance.
long_description:
I cannot walk or stand independently due to paralysis, neuromuscular conditions, injury, congenital differences, or chronic illness. I may use a wheelchair (manual or powered), mobility device, or human assistance for movement. My cognitive abilities are typical unless affected by an unrelated condition. My environment needs to be physically accessible—ramps, elevators, reachable surfaces, and adaptive tools.

I can navigate space efficiently when environments are accessible and well-designed. My experience of movement is primarily through wheels or assistance, not bipedal locomotion. I may have typical sensation or reduced sensation depending on the cause.
